title: Forest Blizzard (APT28/GRU) SOHO Router DNS Hijacking and AiTM Campaign: Russian Military Intelligence Exploits TP-Link and MikroTik Vulnerabilities to Compromise 5,000+ Devices and Steal Credentials from 200+ Organizations Across 120 Countries — FBI Operation Masquerade Disrupts Infrastructure

sources.name: Microsoft Security Blog — SOHO Router Compromise Leads to DNS Hijacking and AiTM Attacks
The Hacker News — Russian State-Linked APT28 Exploits SOHO Routers in Global DNS Hijacking Campaign
SecurityWeek — US Disrupts Russian Espionage Operation Involving Hacked Routers and DNS Hijacking
US Department of Justice — Court-Authorized Disruption of DNS Hijacking Network Controlled by Russian GRU
FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center — Russian GRU Exploiting Vulnerable Routers PSA260407
UK NCSC — APT28 Exploits Routers to Enable DNS Hijacking Operations
Malwarebytes — Russian State-Sponsored Hackers Hijack SOHO Routers for Espionage
The Register — Russia's APT28 Behind Latest Wave of Router and DNS Attacks
CybersecurityNews — FBI Disrupts Russian Router Hijacking Operation
Security Boulevard — Russian Hacking Group Targets SOHO Routers to Spy on Users
DataBreaches.Net — Russians Hijacking Routers for Cyber Spying
Hypertext — How Home and Office Routers Are Being Hacked for Surveillance

timeline.event: CVE-2023-50224 publicly disclosed in TP-Link SOHO routers, providing a known exploitation path for network device compromise
Forest Blizzard (APT28/GRU Military Unit 26165) begins systematic exploitation of SOHO routers globally, altering DNS settings to route victim traffic through GRU-controlled resolvers
Campaign reaches peak scale: 18,000+ unique compromised IP addresses across 120+ countries, targeting government agencies, foreign affairs ministries, and law enforcement organizations
US and allied intelligence agencies complete attribution and evidence gathering; coordination begins with DOJ and FBI for court-authorized disruption operation

FBI executes Operation Masquerade — court-authorized disruption of GRU DNS hijacking infrastructure; Microsoft Security Blog, UK NCSC, and FBI IC3 simultaneously publish advisories; DOJ announces operation
Follow-up coverage and mitigation guidance published by Malwarebytes, The Register, and security vendors; TP-Link and MikroTik urge immediate firmware updates

simple_summary.what_happened: Russia's military intelligence agency (the GRU), operating as a hacker group called Forest Blizzard (also known as APT28 or Fancy Bear), ran a sophisticated espionage campaign that compromised thousands of home and small office routers. By hacking these routers and changing their DNS settings — the system that translates website names into server addresses — the attackers secretly redirected victims' internet traffic through GRU-controlled servers. This allowed them to intercept credentials as people logged into services like Microsoft Outlook Web Access. The campaign peaked at over 18,000 compromised devices across 120 countries before the FBI disrupted the infrastructure on April 7, 2026 via a court-authorized operation called Operation Masquerade.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations and individuals using TP-Link and MikroTik SOHO routers worldwide are affected. Primary espionage targets include government agencies, foreign affairs ministries, law enforcement bodies, and third-party email providers — particularly those using Microsoft Outlook Web Access. Over 200 organizations and 5,000 consumer devices were directly compromised across 120+ countries.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Forest Blizzard (APT28) scans the internet for vulnerable TP-Link and MikroTik routers using known CVEs such as CVE-2023-50224 and default credentials. Step 2: Attackers exploit the vulnerabilities to gain administrative access to target routers remotely. Step 3: The router's DNS settings are silently changed to point to GRU-controlled malicious DNS resolvers instead of legitimate ones. Step 4: When users on the compromised network visit services like Microsoft Outlook Web Access, they are transparently redirected to convincing spoofed login pages controlled by APT28. Step 5: Victims unknowingly enter their real credentials on the fake pages, which are immediately harvested by the attackers. Step 6: Stolen credentials are used for follow-on espionage — accessing email accounts, internal systems, OAuth tokens, and sensitive government or organizational data for extended periods.
what_you_should_do: Immediately update TP-Link and MikroTik router firmware to the latest patched versions and change all default or weak admin passwords to strong unique credentials

Check your router DHCP/DNS configuration for unauthorized changes to DNS resolver addresses — restore to trusted DNS providers (e.g. 1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8, or your ISP's resolvers) if any unknown IPs are found
Review Microsoft Outlook Web Access and other sensitive service authentication logs for suspicious login attempts or sessions from unfamiliar IP addresses or unexpected geographic locations
Disable remote management on SOHO routers unless strictly required, and restrict admin interface access to trusted internal IP addresses only
Consider implementing DNSSEC and DNS-over-HTTPS on network devices and clients to detect and prevent future DNS manipulation
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This is a confirmed state-sponsored espionage campaign by Russian military intelligence (GRU) targeting governments and organizations in over 120 countries. By compromising ordinary home and office routers — devices that rarely receive security attention — APT28 achieved persistent, invisible access to network traffic at massive scale with minimal risk of detection. The campaign targeted foreign affairs ministries, law enforcement agencies, and email infrastructure, indicating high-level geopolitical intelligence collection. The FBI's court-authorized disruption (Operation Masquerade) underscores the severity, but the underlying router vulnerabilities remain, and new campaign infrastructure could be rebuilt rapidly.

tactics.description: Forest Blizzard exploits known CVEs in internet-exposed TP-Link and MikroTik SOHO routers to gain administrative access to the devices
Attackers modify router DNS settings to redirect victims' traffic through GRU-controlled servers, enabling credential interception via spoofed login pages for services like Outlook Web Access
Victims directed to fake login pages that capture real credentials; the phishing occurs invisibly at the DNS/network level without user awareness
Stolen credentials used to maintain persistent access to target email accounts and downstream organizational systems, enabling long-term espionage
Compromised SOHO routers serve as a transparent proxy point, blending malicious traffic interception with legitimate network activity making detection extremely difficult

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying unauthorized DNS configuration changes on network devices and anomalous outbound DNS queries to non-standard resolvers. Organizations should monitor router configuration baselines and watch for credential use from unexpected locations following any period where DNS manipulation may have occurred.

log_sources.description: Monitor for configuration change events — particularly DHCP/DNS server address modifications — and failed or successful login attempts against router admin interfaces from external IPs
Monitor DNS resolver traffic for queries being sent to unexpected external IPs rather than configured resolvers; detect DNS responses originating from non-standard servers
Review OWA and Exchange Online sign-in logs for authentication from unexpected IP addresses, countries, or user agents that may indicate stolen credential use
Look for outbound connections to unknown DNS resolver IPs and unexpected HTTP/HTTPS traffic patterns from router management interfaces to external hosts

what_to_look_for.indicator: DNS server address changed to non-standard IP
what_to_look_for.description: Router DHCP configuration pointing to DNS resolvers not belonging to your ISP, Google (8.8.8.8), Cloudflare (1.1.1.1), or your organization's internal DNS servers may indicate compromise

what_to_look_for.indicator: DNS queries to unauthorized resolver infrastructure
what_to_look_for.description: Outbound UDP/TCP port 53 traffic destined for IP addresses not in your approved DNS resolver list indicates possible DNS hijacking in progress

what_to_look_for.indicator: OWA or mail service authentication from unusual geolocation
what_to_look_for.description: Successful Outlook Web Access logins from IP addresses in unexpected countries or from known VPN/proxy services may indicate use of harvested credentials
what_to_look_for.log_field: eventid=4624 AND logon_type=3 AND source_country NOT IN [expected_countries]

what_to_look_for.indicator: Router admin interface access from external IP
what_to_look_for.description: HTTP/HTTPS access to router web management interface from IP addresses outside your local network or trusted management range
what_to_look_for.log_field: dest_port IN [80,443,8080,8443] AND dest_ip=[router_mgmt_ip] AND src_ip NOT IN [local_ranges]

immediate_actions.action: Audit and restore router DNS configuration
immediate_actions.description: Log into all TP-Link and MikroTik SOHO routers in your environment and verify the DNS server addresses in DHCP settings. If any DNS IPs are unrecognized, document them and immediately restore to trusted resolvers. Cross-reference suspicious IPs against threat intelligence.

immediate_actions.action: Update router firmware and harden administrative access
immediate_actions.description: Apply the latest firmware updates for all TP-Link and MikroTik devices addressing CVE-2023-50224 and related vulnerabilities. Change all admin passwords from defaults to strong unique values. Disable WAN-side remote management unless strictly required. Restrict admin interface to internal management VLANs only.

immediate_actions.action: Audit Microsoft OWA and email authentication logs for past 90 days
immediate_actions.description: Review authentication logs for Outlook Web Access, Microsoft 365, and other internet-facing email services going back to August 2025 (campaign start). Hunt for successful logins from unusual geolocations, IP addresses, or user agents that may indicate stolen credentials were used.

immediate_actions.action: Force credential resets for potentially exposed accounts
immediate_actions.description: If evidence of DNS manipulation is found or cannot be ruled out, initiate mandatory password resets for all accounts that accessed OWA or corporate email during the potential exposure window. Enforce MFA enrollment to prevent future credential-only attacks.

immediate_actions.action: Report confirmed compromise indicators to CISA and FBI
immediate_actions.description: If your organization identifies routers connected to this campaign, report indicators of compromise to CISA (cisa.gov/report) and the FBI IC3 (ic3.gov). Operation Masquerade disrupted some infrastructure but related activity by Forest Blizzard may continue.
